sentences.text: Well, it was Flemish, but some courses were taught in French.
I spoke no Flemish.
I spoke no French.
I had a very lovely teacher.
She was a wonderful woman.
She put me next to a girl, a Jewish girl.
And her name was Rachel, Rachel, who tried to translate for me in Yiddish, which I also did not understand.
But it was all right.
That sink or swim system, and I caught on.
And I went to that school.
And I told you about the Hebrew school disaster there.
And then, of course, there was constant correspondence with Vienna.
In the meantime, we had learned from one of my mother's sisters that her husband had been killed in Dachau, and that she had received the ashes.
And it fell to my father to write the letter to America, which we have, to tell the children-- my two cousins-- that their father was dead.
And to continue the efforts to get to America, to no avail, of course.
Life was very hard in Belgium.
He worked here and there whenever he could get some clandestine little job.
Oh, fine.
Absolutely fine.
There, there was no-- you know, you walked in the street.
As a matter of fact, I remember loving Antwerp, loving it.
It got colors.
My memory begin to have colors here-- real life colors.
Of reality.
Not that gray cloud, you know.
It was a thriving, thriving city, very pretty, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as far as that is concerned, Then the word my aunt got very sick.
She was in a sanatorium.
She had TB.
So my mother spent a lot of time visiting her.
And I spent a lot of time with my little cousin, whom I babysat, and whom I loved very dearly, of course.
We got very attached.
And I had girlfriends, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Belgian girlfriends.
I couldn't tell you if they were Jewish or not.
This, I couldn't tell you.
Of course, by the beginning of 1940, I was almost 12 years old.
And I must say, I was more into my own life and began to listen a little bit less to the-- to the terrifying stories.
I was less aware of the terror around me than I had been in Vienna.
Because I went to the park, when my mother and father had all confidence in me to go with my little cousin, and go to the park.
And-- until, of course, the war broke out.
And the bombs began to fall.
That was very frightening.
That was May, 1940.
Bombs.
The sirens.
The sirens.
First, the sirens.
The alarm, the sirens.
And then, the noise of the planes.
And then, the whizzing, the screeching of the bombs.
And then the explosion.
And looking out and seeing.
We always were down in the basement, and then seeing the sky light up.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Terrifying.
Just terrifying.
But I had my mother and my father together.
You see, I was always with my mother and father together.
I would make sure that one wouldn't leave, that we were always together.
And then the decision was made to leave, you know, together with all the other refugees who hit the road.
So we took a train.
And it was my parents' idea to try to get to somewhere on the coast of France because they must have heard that there were boats going over to England.
And we took a train.
And the train was bombed.
And we had to sleep.
I know they billeted that whole-- not-- the train was not bombed.
The rail line was bombed.
The train could not continue.
So they billeted us in some place-- some commune, I don't know what it was-- very close to the railroad station.
My father said, I'm not staying here.
They are going to bomb that railroad station.
And a number of people were of the same opinion.
So they opened a museum for us-- because there were many people on those trains, which then backed up.
It was in Kortrijk, Belgium.
And there was a beautiful museum.
And we slept on the floor.
I remember my mother had some kind of a fur coat, which she carried along.
No.
Oh, no.
By that, there was a mixed-- not all Jew.
Belgian people, Jewish people.
This was refugees-- a real exodus.
And we spent that night in the museum.
And I was sleeping wrapped up in my mother's fur coat.
And then, of course, it was bombed, hearing the bombs.
And the next morning, that place was bombed, where we had-- I remember how that impressed me.
And how that impressed me, and how my mother said, you see, prayer, prayer, prayer is the answer here.
Prayer is the answer.
Because my mother prayed all the time.
And I don't think I believed it then, either.
But I don't know.
And then, we somehow got somewhere else and took another train.
And then we got in the direction of Dunkirk.
I can't tell you how far we got.
But then, the train itself was bombed, part of the train.
And that is the worst of my life.
I remember the flesh, the smell of burning flesh.
I remember the panic.
I remember the screaming.
And my father wanted to go, and I wouldn't.
I wouldn't.
The greatest panic was that wherever one went, all three of us had to go together.
It was-- that's why I can't go to any movies where there's violence.
I can't, because it's a reality.
That was a reality.
And somehow, we got out of there, took another train, together with--
We were always together with aunt-- my mother's sister-- and her husband, and the little boy.
No, I did not.
Not personally.
We knew no one.
We knew no one.
We just were together.
And I can't tell you how we got some other train, some other rail lines.
And the word of mouth got around, what to do, where to go.
And of course, they only took army.
They only took soldiers that crossed.
That was, at the time of the debacle, of the French debacle, when row boats and everything went to England.
I subsequently found out that some people did buy their way into some of these boats.
Obviously, we didn't.
And then we got all the way down to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the middle of France.
No, I would say not the middle-- southern France.
And we were refugees from Belgium.
However, quickly, the French authorities separated us from Belgium refugees and made us enemy aliens.
Enemy aliens.
Although my father was born in Poland, he was an enemy alien.
And all Jews were made enemy aliens.
Germans, Austrians, whatever.
And they sort of billeted us in this small town called Montesquieu-sur-Lauragais.
It was a very small village.
And there was a house.
And they gave the refugees a house to live in.
And they served meals, the community.
I think, mainly, the priest.
The priest was very friendly.
Served meals.
And I remember my parents and my aunt and uncle always staying awake all night.
Somebody was-- it was like on guard duty.
Somebody always stayed awake.
They took turns being awake at night in case they should come for us.
And then, sure enough, they came for the men in a truck.
The French gendarmerie.
The French gendarmerie.
That was near Toulouse.
Montesquieu-sur-Lauragais.
That was near Toulouse.
And they took them.
And we had no idea where they were.
No idea where the men had gone.
And then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through the grapevine-- wait.
No, that's not quite how it went.
We were in that Montesquieu.
And the grapevine somehow had it that they would come.
They would come for the men.
And again, we hit a train trying to get-- it was said that if you were able to penetrate into Spain, they would not reject you.
The Spanish didn't, which is the truth, which is absolutely a fact that history has proven.
So we hit a train.
And we went, trying to get to the Spanish border.
And it was in Perpignan.
Perpignan, Perpignan.
But they made everyone come off the train and asked for papers.
This was still in France?
Oh, yes.
We're in France.
So there was my father, my mother, and I, my uncle, my aunt, and my little cousin, and a relative of the brother-in-law.
His name was Menkus.
I don't know more about him.
He was a man by himself.
And in Perpignan, they made us leave the train and asked for papers.
And, of course, my uncle and my father were-- the Jews have no papers, you know, then and there.
And all these men were taken away.
And there we were in Perpignan, left on the station platform.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't-- my recollection goes blank as to how we got all places to Montpellier, which is in Herault, which is further east.
My mother, and my aunt, and I.
And then the big search for the men started, because, by that time, we had heard there were these cabs.
[FRENCH]
So we tried to go close to Saint-Cyprien, were stopped, and thrown into-- my aunt, and my mother, and my little cousin, and I were thrown into a detention camp called Argeles-sur-Mer.
And it was barracks built for the Spanish refugees.
And the camp was guarded by-- it was the first time in my life I saw a Black person.
The guards would tall, handsome Senegalese men.
They were very kind.
And I remember them encouraging men who were there to just get in to the sea and to swim to Spain.
And I think some of them did.
Whether they succeeded-- but they sure hit the water.
I remember that because the only way of keeping clean and not getting the lice and all kinds of vermin was to just go into the Mediterranean.
This was on-- the barracks were on the sand.
Yes.
It was surrounded by barbed wire and guards.
Barracks with cots.
The barracks were roomy.
The cots were lined up on either side.
There was no food.
There was no food.
It was just-- but there was tomatoes.
Gave us tomatoes, grapes, whatever was in the region.
And the only way to get, really, food was to bribe a guard.
I remember my mother having some dollar bills and giving $1 so we got-- she got tea leave-- tea leaves.
So with the same tea leaves, we had tea for I don't know how long.
And of course, people got dysentery and all kinds of gastrointestinal ailments, together with lice, and so on.
My little cousin was the first to get dysentery.
And there was one barrack that was separated from the main barracks by barbed wire, which was the infirmary.
And he was taken into the infirmary.
And the following day, I followed because I came down with it, too.
But I was-- he was little and not so strong.
And I was a big girl and much stronger.
And I noticed that just before the children died-- and as a matter of fact, in the book, it tells you how many children died in Argeles.
Three months old.
Four months old.
One year old.
It was only a children's infirmary.
And I noticed that the more they had diarrhea, the more their little potty under the bed was full, the quicker they would die.
And I also noticed that the personnel, when they noticed that the child was about to die, would just let the child die.
So what I did is I remember changing potties under my little' cousin.
I was not as sick as my little cousin was.
I really was not.
And I was more-- I guess more assertive at the time.
He was six years younger.
We are now 1940, so I was 12, and he was 6
Danny.
Daniel.
And the parents could come once a day to talk across.
And I explained to my mother what was happening, and to my aunt.
And I said, you've got to-- you've got to get us out because this little boy, this little girl, just died.
So I saw these little-- and it's amazing.
I was not-- I was not at all as scared as when the bombs would fall.
I was kind of there.
I had to get out.
I had to get out of this infirmary.
My little cousin and I had to get out.
And we did.
Did they do anything to treat you for the ailment?
I can't remember.
Well, even if they tried, there was nothing to treat us with.
I mean there was nothing to treat us with.
I remember they were-- they had white uniforms.
I remember that.
I remember they were kind, really.
But I don't think there was anything to treat us with.
So then, my mother got us out.
I remember, with those tea bags, with just feeding us liquids, instinctively-- whether my mother and aunt knew, or didn't know, did it as mothers-- we got back on our feet.
And then, of course, as I said, they were bribable.
And I think we somehow had one $10 bill, or what-- $20?
I think it must have been one $10.
But the wonderful thing about American money is that all denominations are the same size.
My aunt was very inventive.
She put that $20 bill on top.
And she put a few ones underneath.
And she went with me.
And I was the spokesperson because, by that time, I spoke French.
And we went to the commander, or whoever he was.
No.
He was in uniform.
But he was a Frenchman.
And my aunt said, look, we have all this money.
And we're not going to be a burden to anybody.
And we will make it worth your while, if-- our children are sick, to just let us out.
And he did.
He said, I'll open the gate.
And I remember, it was my mother.
My mother said, do you think ever in my life, I'll have-- I remember her words, [NON-ENGLISH].
Bread, and butter, and coffee.
There was absolutely nothing to eat.
And we found a farmer.
It was a donkey.
It was not a pony.
It was a donkey with a cart.
We couldn't walk.
We really couldn't walk.
We were so weak.
We couldn't walk.
In Argeles?
Not too long.
Maybe a couple of months.
Really, not too long.
I would say two months.
We came there in June of 1940-- end of June.
And we must have been out by August.
It was not that-- you see, it was not-- you had to be inventive a little bit.
My aunt was very inventive.
No.
No.
No.
I personally saw nothing.
No, I just saw terrible hunger, disease, and desperation.
But no punishment, no corporal-- And I remember these very tall, very broad Senegalese as being extremely kind.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I think because they were discriminated against by the French, so they kind of felt a kinship.
I remember that vaguely, that they felt a kinship with-- with the prisoners.
But there was nowhere to go.
The barracks were in the sand.
On three sides, there were barbed wire.
The people were very weak.
I'm sure a number of them escaped.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, I remember reading profile of Felix Roden.
He was there at the same time, the same thing.
But these people had much more money and were successful to go into Spain.
So that's how we got out of Argeles, and somehow made our way to Montpellier, and then found our father.
Where was he?
Saint-Cyprien.
He had-- they had been thrown into Saint-Cyprien, which was very close to Argeles.
Yes.
And then, as he writes, in October of 1940, he was taken from Saint-Cyprien to Gurs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all efforts of liberating him were futile.
And my father had dysentery.
Men died.
That is where the OSE came in.
There were many children.
They were taken in with their families.
There were convoys had come from Germany with children into Gurs, and Gurs and Rivesaltes.
And the OSE, together with the Quakers, had permission-- don't forget, this was all now legal-- had permission to provide nursing care and social services in those camps.
I was with my mother in Montpellier.
This is the fall of 1940.
School-- Lycee Louis Blanc.
My mother took me to school.
And she, in order to eat, she had gone to the synagogue, and refugees, and she got jobs making dresses, sewing for families.
And then I got an attack of appendicitis.
And whatever doctor came didn't know it and told my mother put a hot water bottle on me.
And that was it.
So I remember the ambulance taking to the municipal hospital.
And it was bad.
I had peritonitis.
And in those days, there were no antibiotics.
So my mother had me in the hospital with peritonitis and my father in Gurs with dysentery.
And the German victories were, of course, proclaimed every day over the radio and the newspaper.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know. whatever.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She made contact in Montpellier synagogue.
In the meantime, the OSE had moved its headquarters to Montpellier.
It stands for Oeuvre de Secours aux Enfants.
And it began as a self-help Jewish organization in Russia because of the poverty of Russian Jews.
And intellectuals and physicians had formed the-- it's a long history, but it started in Russia, then after the communist takeover, moved to Berlin.
And in 1933, moved to Paris.
And then after the Armistice, moved on to Montpellier.
And their task was to give medical and social services to Jewish children.
Yeah, my mother met these-- yes, she met them in the synagogue.
And these people persuaded her to send me-- because I was in the hospital a long time.
I had to have physical therapy to walk.
And I was devastated.
I didn't get well very quickly because I was so worried about my mother and my father.
And I must say, in the municipal hospital of Montpellier, there was one sister who took care of me.
And she really pulled me through.
She-- she would sit, of course, with the rosary.
And I remember my mother coming one day and saying, please, Sister, don't.
Don't do that.
And I remember what she looked like.
And she said, I expect you to pray to your God, please let me pray to mine.
And we'll succeed.
And she used to read to me.
She read me all these French children's literature in the hospital.
Soeur [FRENCH].
I corresponded with her until the '50s when she died.
Yeah.
That's one person I kept in touch with.
Well, corresponded, I sent one letter a year, and she sent a letter back.
I remember I used to see the entrance to the hospital grounds.
And I knew what time they would open the gate.
And my mother was always there.
She had always taken the trolley a little bit ahead of time to be one of the first to come in because that's what I was waiting for every single day.
And my aunt came to visit, too.
And after that, so in January of 1941, they sent me to Chabannes.
Chabannes was a children's home not far from Limoges.
[FRENCH].
Not far from Clermont-Ferrand.
Where's Clermont-Ferrand?
OK.
North of Limoges.
Everyone heard of Limoges.
North of Limoges.
Northeast of Limoges.
There were many.

I want to point out that there were many of these children's homes in that region because that was a region with a history of anti-clerical sentiment, of being a sort of a revolutionary, non-traditional French peasant population.
And they took in the Jewish children.
Many Jewish families survived in that particular region, as they did in that Protestant village because of-- but this was not.
This was just, as somebody remarked, when you come into that region of France, suddenly you don't see so many churches.
That's true.
You don't.
Horrible.
Horrible.
The counselor who was in charge of me, and who later after the war, she said I cried every night for 41 nights.
She said she counted.
I cried myself to sleep for 41 consecutive nights.
I had forgotten that.
I just know I was as miserable as can be.
I did not want to participate in anything.
I just wanted to put the sheet over my head and stay in my bed.
So it took a month and a half.
And they were-- well, the counselors and the teachers were a mixed bag.
There were some who were professional pedagogues, who were wonderful and very good.
And then there were some, who, through some way or another, got themself the safe job in a safe environment, and who were not so good.
The funny thing is, I knew it then instinctively.
There was one who was a dyed-in-the-wool communist.
And the mandate was to keep our Jewishness alive.
Not, perhaps, in the theological, religious manner, but observing and so on.
And he was really subversive.
He was a true, convicted communist.
And he was very clever, too.
But I mean this is just a sort of a profile of how-- but, generally, the people were wonderful.
It was run by a man by the name of Maurice Chevrier, who would be a Freemason, and who was very good to us, very good to these Jewish children.
